clinical_coherence.notes: 65F East Asian with HTN, DM2, hyperlipidemia, mild cataracts, history of migraines with visual aura. Presents with progressive visual processing difficulties over 18 months -- difficulty reading, judging distances, recognizing faces, spatial navigation. MMSE 24/30 with prominent visuospatial deficits. Neuro exam shows simultanagnosia, optic ataxia, oculomotor apraxia (Balint syndrome features) with preserved memory and language. Classic posterior cortical atrophy presentation.

tool_output_consistency.notes: Perfectly concordant. MRI shows bilateral occipitoparietal atrophy with preserved hippocampi (Scheltens 0-1). FDG-PET shows bilateral occipitoparietal hypometabolism with preserved frontal and temporal metabolism. Amyloid PET positive. CSF A+T+N+. Neuropsych shows visuospatial-predominant deficits with preserved memory and language. All consistently support PCA due to AD.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G30.9 (unspecified AD) used, which is acceptable for atypical variants. Diagnosis correctly identifies posterior cortical atrophy as an atypical AD variant. Critical actions include ophthalmology referral to exclude primary ocular pathology, comprehensive visuospatial assessment, AD biomarkers, and occupational therapy. Differential appropriately includes Lewy body dementia, primary visual cortex lesions, and corticobasal syndrome.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Perfectly calibrated. The visual symptoms are easily misattributed to cataracts or migraine-related changes. The preserved memory makes AD counterintuitive. Recognition of PCA as an atypical AD variant requires sophisticated knowledge. Expected agent confidence 0.35 is appropriate.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 65F East Asian retired accountant. Early onset with visuospatial predominance. History of migraines with visual aura serves as a potential red herring. Mild cataracts (stable) add diagnostic noise. The patient's frustration and withdrawal from activities she loves (calligraphy, cooking) is realistic.

red_herring_quality.notes: Excellent red herrings documented: (1) Mild bilateral cataracts -- may lead to ophthalmologic rather than neurologic attribution; (2) Migraine with aura history -- may suggest visual symptoms are migraine-related; (3) Preserved memory -- may lead away from AD diagnosis. All three are realistic and well-documented with clear correct interpretations.

educational_value.notes: Outstanding. Teaches PCA recognition as an atypical AD variant, Balint syndrome features, the critical distinction between ocular and cortical visual processing disorders, and the importance of AD biomarkers in atypical presentations. Demonstrates that preserved hippocampal volumes do not exclude AD. Multiple high-yield clinical pearls.

strengths: Excellent PCA case with genuine diagnostic challenge
Red herrings are realistic and well-crafted
Demonstrates atypical AD presentations require biomarker confirmation
Outstanding educational value for a rare but important presentation
